page.content: The analysis of exon position and alternative splice choice reveals various statistical outcomes across the RefSeq, KCl, and TPG databases. For the initial 5' splice choice, the percentage in the RefSeq database is 24.32, while in KCl exon it is 28.14, with a p-value of 1.367 × e-03* and a TPG exon percentage of 27.70 and a p-value of 8.081 × e-05*. The alternative start shows 25.25 in RefSeq, 27.18 in KCl, with a p-value of 6.386 × e-02* and a TPG percentage of 30.07 and a p-value of 6.674 × e-08*. The internal 5' shows 5.31, 8.02, 1.179 × e-13*, 6.53, and 4.935 × e-11*.

The Wish model relationship continuum componentsThe relationship ynamic
Forensic community mental health team (CMHT) healthcare professionals (HCPs)General Adult CMHT HCPs
Symmetric – AsymmetricPower dependence centredness agencyGreater sense of partnership for both clozapine service users (CSUs) and HCP. Themes centred around the CSU rather than the HCP. CSUs have gained greater agency and reduced dependence on HCPs. Result: perception of a symmetrical HCP/CSU relationship.HCPs complete activities either for or on behalf of CSU. Themes centred on HCPs rather than CSU. CSU appears to be highly dependent on the HCP. Result: perception of an asymmetrical HCP/CSU relationship.
Cooperative –CompetitiveValence agreement communication conflictHCP and CSU agendas aligned to increase independence and normalisation. Engagement in shared care provided an opportunity to achieve their shared agendas. HCPs trust in CSUs ability to perform own role in shared care. Result: perception of a cooperative HCP/CSU relationship.No evidence of agenda alignment. Formal medical knowledge competing with lay knowledge. Some HCPs described lacking trust in CSU relationships. Result: perception of a competitive component to HCP/CSU relationship.
Intimate – DistantIntensity transactional/relational commitmentMonitoring prescription collection transactional element but in context of a relational focus on a CSU long-term goals. Forensic HCPs demonstrated commitment through motivation and willingness to adapt their beliefs and behaviours to fulfil the shared agenda of independence. CSUs committed to their own role in shared care. Result: perception of an intimate HCP/CSU relationship.Adult HCPs talked about undertaking activities on behalf of the CSU with no reference to the future aims of the CSU. Adult HCPs demonstrated little change in beliefs and behaviours in a shared-care role of the CSUs, suggesting a distant relationship with little commitment for the potentially longer term goals of the CSU. Result: perception of a transactional and distant HCP/CSU relationship.

Social affect aided by the commitment to HCP–CSU relationship. Result: perception of a more social professional HCP/CSU relationship.Considerable professional formality. Professional affect demonstrated by the power differential between the HCP and CSU. Result: perception of a formal professional HCP/CSU relationship.

cells.cell_text: The Wish model relationship continuum components
Forensic community mental health team (CMHT) healthcare professionals (HCPs)

Greater sense of partnership for both clozapine service users (CSUs) and HCP. Themes centred around the CSU rather than the HCP. CSUs have gained greater agency and reduced dependence on HCPs. Result: perception of a symmetrical HCP/CSU relationship.

HCPs complete activities either for or on behalf of CSU. Themes centred on HCPs rather than CSU. CSU appears to be highly dependent on the HCP. Result: perception of an asymmetrical HCP/CSU relationship.

HCP and CSU agendas aligned to increase independence and normalisation. Engagement in shared care provided an opportunity to achieve their shared agendas. HCPs trust in CSUs ability to perform own role in shared care. Result: perception of a cooperative HCP/CSU relationship.

No evidence of agenda alignment. Formal medical knowledge competing with lay knowledge. Some HCPs described lacking trust in CSU relationships. Result: perception of a competitive component to HCP/CSU relationship.

Monitoring prescription collection transactional element but in context of a relational focus on a CSU long-term goals. Forensic HCPs demonstrated commitment through motivation and willingness to adapt their beliefs and behaviours to fulfil the shared agenda of independence. CSUs committed to their own role in shared care. Result: perception of an intimate HCP/CSU relationship.

Adult HCPs talked about undertaking activities on behalf of the CSU with no reference to the future aims of the CSU. Adult HCPs demonstrated little change in beliefs and behaviours in a shared-care role of the CSUs, suggesting a distant relationship with little commitment for the potentially longer term goals of the CSU. Result: perception of a transactional and distant HCP/CSU relationship.

Engaging in shared care enabled a shift in formality dimension to social as the relationship extends into primary care. Social affect aided by the commitment to HCP–CSU relationship. Result: perception of a more social professional HCP/CSU relationship.
Considerable professional formality. Professional affect demonstrated by the power differential between the HCP and CSU. Result: perception of a formal professional HCP/CSU relationship.

page.content: The internal 3' has 5.32, 8.05, 8.393 × e-14*, 6.77, and 9.659 × e-15*. The final alternative termination reports 23.43, 25.63, 1.574 × e-02*, 28.55, and 1.110 × e-16*, while the final 3' shows 22.92, 23.27, 3.515 × e-01, 24.83, and 7.685 × e-04*. In the Wish model relationship continuum, the symmetric-asymmetric dynamic indicates a greater sense of partnership for both clozapine service users (CSUs) and HCPs, with themes centred around the CSU rather than the HCP, resulting in a perception of a symmetrical HCP/CSU relationship. Conversely, HCPs complete activities either for or on behalf of CSUs, centring themes on HCPs rather than CSUs, indicating a perception of an asymmetrical HCP/CSU relationship. The cooperative-competitive dynamic reveals that HCP and CSU agendas align to increase independence and normalisation, resulting in a perception of a cooperative HCP/CSU relationship. However, there is no evidence of agenda alignment; formal medical knowledge competes with lay knowledge, leading to a perception of a competitive component in the HCP/CSU relationship. The intimate-distant dynamic shows monitoring of prescription collection as a transactional element within a relational focus on CSU long-term goals, demonstrating HCP commitment through motivation to adapt beliefs for shared independence, resulting in an intimate HCP/CSU relationship. In contrast, adult HCPs perform activities on behalf of the CSU without reference to future aims, indicating a distant relationship with minimal commitment, perceived as transactional. Lastly, in the social-professional dynamic, engaging in shared care shifts the relationship from formal to social as it extends into primary care, resulting in a perception of a more social professional HCP/CSU relationship, while considerable professional formality persists, illustrating a power differential and resulting in a perception of a formal professional HCP/CSU relationship.
